TextBlock.Text: SOVIET COMMERCE RAIDERS TIE UP IN INDIAN HARBOR AMIDST WESTERN DIPLOMATIC FURY
India, officially neutral, accused of playing favorites

StackPanel.TextBlock:
COCHIN, INDIA — A Soviet commerce raiding naval group entered the Indian port of Cochin yesterday, according to officials in New Delhi and several Western capitals, bringing an end to a weeks-long pursuit by U.S. and Allied naval forces across the South Atlantic and Indian Ocean.
U.S. defense officials confirmed that American warships and aircraft were forced to break off their pursuit as the Soviet vessels approached Indian territorial waters, citing New Delhi's declaration of neutrality. Western diplomats have expressed extreme frustration with India's decision, while Indian officials maintained that the move was necessary to prevent further escalation and bloodshed in their regional waters.
Defense analysts around the world commented that the raiding campaign had compelled NATO to divert significant naval assets from primary theaters. (continued on page 2)

TextBlock.Text: The Soviet heavy missile cruiser Petropavlovsk on exercise from a pre-war photo. This cruiser and its captain traversed Atlantic and Indian oceans, hiding from NATO forces and striking seemingly at will. Defense analysts believe this forced the diversion of significant NATO assets from primary theaters. A U.S. senator told Exposition News that the decision to halt operations as the Soviet force entered Indian waters represented “a serious failure of resolve,” though U.S. officials declined further comment, citing India’s declared neutrality. U.S. and Allied warships remain at sea off the Indian coast. (DoD Photo)
U.S. FORMS MARITIME BLOCKADE OFF INDIA

StackPanel.TextBlock:
COCHIN, INDIA - U.S. naval forces established a maritime barrier off India’s western coast today, positioning surface ships and patrol aircraft outside Indian territorial waters as Soviet warships remain interned in Cochin harbor. Defense officials said the deployment is intended to maintain surveillance while diplomatic efforts continue.
Military sources reported that carrier-based aircraft and escort vessels are conducting continuous monitoring of maritime approaches to the port, with commanders emphasizing adherence to India’s declared exclusion zone.
Although no additional military action has been authorized, U.S. planners cautioned that forces will remain on station as long as the Soviet presence persists. Allied officials said the barrier reflects concern over potential redeployment of the raider force, even as attention shifts back toward primary theaters. (continued on page 3)

TextBlock.Text: A U.S. Navy Orion patrol plane flies over a surfaced submarine, part of a show of force intended to keep the Soviets at anchor in Cochin. U.S. defense officials maintain they are acting with full respect for India's neutrality. (U.S. Navy photo)
